title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While examining high-quality ceramics in a shop, Mr. Satterthwaite reminisces about the late Duchess Maria Lis, recalling her kindness toward her maid during a voyage to Corsica and her preference for brownish-purple Roquefort tea sets. After deciding to purchase a vase, the sun emerges from behind the clouds, illuminating a Gothic-style stained-glass window. The light casts a colorful mosaic onto the back of a tall, muscular man in a black suit who has just sat down in the café.

Recognizing the man's silhouette, Mr. Satterthwaite approaches him and identifies him as Mr. Quine (also referred to as Mr. Queene or Mr. Quincannon). The two men engage in a cryptic conversation regarding their past and the influence Quine has had on Satterthwaite's actions. Quine claims he is merely passing through Kingsburn Dacey to visit an old friend who is recovering from a stroke and living with family. 

Quine introduces his small black dog, Hermes, who signals a young man named Ali to bring two cups of Turkish coffee. The conversation turns to a past "tragic scene" involving a death. Quine argues that death is not always a tragedy and emphasizes that when given a mission, they strive to save lives. When Satterthwaite asks if he is being given a mission, Quine denies it, stating he does not meddle in others' affairs and that Satterthwaite will always know how to observe and act on his own.
